title: Students For Life Advocates Legal Do-It-Yourself Abortions
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman critiques Students for Life and its president Kristan Hawkins for promoting abortion laws that grant blanket immunity to mothers, arguing this position perpetuates unequal justice for preborn victims and leaves do-it-yourself abortion fully legal.
audiences: Abolitionist seeking sharper arguments against immunity provisions
Legal-minded reader interested in equal protection arguments
Newcomer trying to distinguish abolitionism from pro-life incrementalism

questions.question: What position is Students for Life formally advocating in their new presentation and flyer?
questions.audience: Newcomer trying to distinguish abolitionism from pro-life incrementalism
questions.answer: Students for Life is teaching that abortion laws should hold only the abortionist criminally liable for the child's death, leaving the mother immune from prosecution. Kristan Hawkins, the group's president, is touring the country to promote this view as the proper pro-life legal framework.
questions.quote: Students for Life has a new presentation and flyer which explains their position that laws against abortion should make only the abortionist liable for prosecution for the child’s death. Kirstan Hawkins, the president of Students for Life is currently touring the United States presenting on this topic.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call mother-immunity laws fundamentally unjust?
questions.audience: Abolitionist seeking sharper arguments against immunity provisions
questions.answer: Because granting blanket immunity to one class of participants in the killing of a preborn child—the mother—creates two tiers of justice for human victims, treating the preborn as less worthy of legal protection than the born. A consistent law would simply apply existing murder statutes to all human beings without carve-outs.
questions.quote: A just abortion law would simply be the current law against murder applied to all human beings. The justice applied to someone who plays a part in the murder of a born person would be the same justice applied to someone involved in the murder of a preborn person. That would be justice.

questions.question: How did pro-life immunity provisions contribute to the reasoning of Roe v. Wade?
questions.audience: Legal-minded reader interested in equal protection arguments
questions.answer: Justice Harry Blackmun seized on the inconsistency in the Texas statute—which simultaneously claimed the fetus was a person but exempted the mother from being a principal or accomplice and set lower penalties than for murder—as evidence the law did not actually treat the preborn as full persons. Pro-life hypocrisy, in Silberman's reading, helped supply the reasoning Blackmun used to strike down protective laws.
questions.quote: It’s pro-life hypocrisy on this matter that led to Roe v Wade in the first place. Roe author Justice Harry Blackmun wrote that the Texas law in question which gave blanket immunity to the mother was not consistent with the other claims of the pro-life defenders of that law.

questions.question: What inconsistency did Blackmun specifically identify in Texas's abortion statute?
questions.audience: Legal-minded reader interested in equal protection arguments
questions.answer: Blackmun pointed out that Texas refused to treat the woman as a principal or accomplice to abortion, and that the criminal penalty for abortion was significantly lower than for murder. He argued these features were impossible to square with the claim that the fetus was a person entitled to Fourteenth Amendment protection.
questions.quote: If the fetus is a person, why is the woman not a principal or an accomplice? Further, the penalty for criminal abortion specified by Art. 1195 is significantly less than the maximum penalty for murder prescribed by Art. 1257 of the Texas Penal Code. If the fetus is a person, may the penalties be different?

questions.question: What does Silberman say is at stake if pro-life laws themselves refuse to treat the preborn as human beings?
questions.answer: He argues the entire moral case for ending abortion collapses into incoherence. If the people writing the laws will not afford full legal protection to preborn children, the public has no reason to take seriously the claim that the preborn deserve such protection.
questions.quote: If pro-life laws and the pro-life leaders who write them don’t treat the preborn child like a human being, why would anyone else? We must demand equal justice for preborn human beings. To not do so is to be incoherent so as to betray our the foundation of our case: that the victims of abortion are due full protection of the law.

questions.question: Why does Silberman claim the pro-life movement is not actually about criminalizing abortion?
questions.audience: Newcomer trying to distinguish abolitionism from pro-life incrementalism
questions.answer: Because the movement is committed to treating the mother as a victim and granting her automatic immunity, its proposed laws never criminalize abortion as an act—they only criminalize the act of an abortionist performing one. The mother's choice to procure or perform an abortion remains lawful.
questions.quote: Most people probably think that the pro-life movement is about criminalizing abortion. This is incorrect. The pro-life movement is dogmatically wedded to the idea that the parents and especially the mother who has her child murdered is a victim. Thus, they grant immunity from prosecution to the mother in all pro-life laws.

questions.question: What concrete loophole does mother-immunity create in any 'pro-life' statute?
questions.audience: Abolitionist seeking sharper arguments against immunity provisions
questions.answer: It leaves do-it-yourself abortion entirely legal. The statute reaches only licensed abortionists assisting in a killing; a mother who self-induces—by pill, instrument, or any other means—commits no crime under such a regime.
questions.quote: Such a law does not criminalize abortion, it merely criminalizes abortion performed by a licensed abortionist. A pro-life law that gives automatic immunity to the mother in all cases does nothing about the legality of abortion itself. All it does is *make it illegal for an abortionist to help a mother murder her baby*. Do-it-yourself abortion is still perfectly legal under pro-life laws.

questions.question: How does Silberman respond to the historical claim that American law has never prosecuted parents for having their children aborted?
questions.answer: He concedes the historical fact but rejects it as a guide for the future. As Roe footnote 54 itself shows, that legacy was used to undermine the personhood of the preborn—it is a precedent of injustice, not a model worth preserving.
questions.quote: On their flier, Students for Life points out that the American legal system has never prosecuted mothers and fathers for having their children murdered. They’re right, but as demonstrated by Roe footnote 54, that legacy of foolishness and injustice is not an example to return to but a terrible mistake to learn from.

questions.question: What additional resource does the article point readers to for a fuller treatment of these arguments?
questions.answer: Silberman directs readers to a presentation by Texas family attorney Bradley Pierce, delivered at the 2018 Church Arise Conference, which examines the equal-justice and DIY-abortion problems with mother-immunity laws in greater depth.
questions.quote: The below presentation from Texas family attorney Bradley Pierce from the 2018 Church Arise Conference delves deeply and articulately into all of these matters.

related.title: Kristan Hawkins’ Flawed Reasoning vs Scripture
related.reason: Direct follow-up critique of the same Students for Life president whose mother-immunity tour this article responds to.

related.title: A Mother Is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
related.reason: Develops the moral and legal case against treating the mother as a categorical victim—the exact premise this article attacks.

related.title: Criminalization
related.reason: Lays out the abolitionist position on equal-protection criminalization that Silberman is contrasting with the pro-life model.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Frames the broader distinction the article relies on—why granting immunity makes the pro-life movement something other than genuinely abolitionist.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: The DIY-abortion loophole identified here is one of the structural failings of the pro-life incremental approach catalogued in this companion piece.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Extends the same Texas-statute critique Blackmun used in Roe, showing how mother-immunity language continues to gut nominally pro-life laws.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Explains the ideological commitments—including mother-as-victim dogma—that put pro-life leadership at odds with the equal-justice argument made here.
